TEXT.citation: Wikipedia contributors. (2026). Sakizaya Wikipedia. Wikimedia Foundation. Retrieved 2026-06-09, from https://szy.wikipedia.org/
TEXT.BibTeX_citation: @misc{Wiki_szy, title = {Sakizaya Wikipedia}, author = {{Wikipedia contributors}}, year = {2026}, publisher = {Wikimedia Foundation}, url = {https://szy.wikipedia.org/}, urldate = {2026-06-09} }

S.FORM: == insin == ťäÄśĚŹ u siwkay nu nipaluma mahtikay ku papah tabakiay a kilang. talakaw tina kilang. maka 25-40m ku talakaw. ihekalay a heci singangan ku "sangelacay a heci ÁôĹśěú". matenes ku uzip tina kilang, cacay katukuh tulu malebut mihcaan ku uzip. u sanek nu nipaluma malaheci tu imelang pasubana i cacudadan a lacul u zuma a cidekay a kamu, saungay misapayu' a cidekay u sulit nu zuma a kamu-Hulam atu Amilika Ginkgo biloba, commonly known as ginkgo or gingko , also known as the ginkgo tree or the maidenhair tree, is the only living species in the division Ginkgophyta, all others being extinct. It is found in fossils dating back 270 million years. Native to China, the tree is widely cultivated, and was cultivated early in human history. It has various uses in traditional medicine and as a source of food. The genus name Ginkgo is regarded as a misspelling of the Japanese gin kyo, "silver apricot".
S.PHON: == insin == ** u siwkaj nu nipar~…ĺuma mańßtikaj ku papańß tabakiaj a kir~…ĺaŇč. tar~…ĺakaw tina kir~…ĺaŇč. maka **-**m ku tar~…ĺakaw. ińß…ôkar~…ĺaj a ńß…ô ¶i siŇčaŇčan ku "saŇč…ôr~…ĺa ¶aj a ńß…ô ¶i **". mat…ôn…ôs ku uzip tina kir~…ĺaŇč, ¶a ¶aj katukuńß tur~…ĺu mar~…ĺ…ôbut mińß ¶aan ku uzip. u san…ôk nu nipar~…ĺuma mar~…ĺańß…ô ¶i tu im…ôr~…ĺaŇč pasubana i ¶a ¶udadan a r~…ĺa ¶ur~…ĺ u zuma a ¶id…ôkaj a kamu, sauŇčaj misapaju Ē~ ° a ¶id…ôkaj u sur~…ĺit nu zuma a kamu-ńßur~…ĺam atu amir~…ĺika ginkgo bir~…ĺoba, ¶ommonr~…ĺj known as ginkgo or giŇčko , ar~…ĺso known as tńß…ô ginkgo tr…ô…ô or tńß…ô maid…ônńßair tr…ô…ô, is tńß…ô onr~…ĺj r~…ĺi*iŇč sp…ô ¶i…ôs in tńß…ô di*ision ginkgopńßjta, ar~…ĺr~…ĺ otńß…ôrs b…ôiŇč …ô*tin ¶t. it is found in fossir~…ĺs datiŇč ba ¶k *** mir~…ĺr~…ĺion j…ôars. nati*…ô to ¶ńßina, tńß…ô tr…ô…ô is wid…ôr~…ĺj ¶ur~…ĺti*at…ôd, and was ¶ur~…ĺti*at…ôd …ôarr~…ĺj in ńßuman ńßistorj. it ńßas *arious us…ôs in traditionar~…ĺ m…ôdi ¶in…ô and as a sour ¶…ô of food. tńß…ô g…ônus nam…ô ginkgo is r…ôgard…ôd as a missp…ôr~…ĺr~…ĺiŇč of tńß…ô *apan…ôs…ô gin kjo, "sir~…ĺ*…ôr apri ¶ot".
S.FORM: == insin == ťäÄśĚŹ u siwkay nu nipaluma mahtikay ku papah tabakiay a kilang. talakaw tina kilang. maka 25-40m ku talakaw. ihekalay a heci singangan ku "sangelacay a heci ÁôĹśěú". matenes ku uzip tina kilang, cacay katukuh tulu malebut mihcaan ku uzip. u sanek nu nipaluma malaheci tu imelang pasubana i cacudadan a lacul u zuma a cidekay a kamu, saungay misapayu' a cidekay u sulit nu zuma a kamu-Hulam atu Amilika Ginkgo biloba, commonly known as ginkgo or gingko , also known as the ginkgo tree or the maidenhair tree, is the only living species in the division Ginkgophyta, all others being extinct. It is found in fossils dating back 270 million years. Native to China, the tree is widely cultivated, and was cultivated early in human history. It has various uses in traditional medicine and as a source of food. The genus name Ginkgo is regarded as a misspelling of the Japanese gin kyo, "silver apricot".
S.PHON: == insin == ** u siwkaj nu nipar~…ĺuma mańßtikaj ku papańß tabakiaj a kir~…ĺaŇč. tar~…ĺakaw tina kir~…ĺaŇč. maka **-**m ku tar~…ĺakaw. ińß…ôkar~…ĺaj a ńß…ô ¶i siŇčaŇčan ku "saŇč…ôr~…ĺa ¶aj a ńß…ô ¶i **". mat…ôn…ôs ku uzip tina kir~…ĺaŇč, ¶a ¶aj katukuńß tur~…ĺu mar~…ĺ…ôbut mińß ¶aan ku uzip. u san…ôk nu nipar~…ĺuma mar~…ĺańß…ô ¶i tu im…ôr~…ĺaŇč pasubana i ¶a ¶udadan a r~…ĺa ¶ur~…ĺ u zuma a ¶id…ôkaj a kamu, sauŇčaj misapaju Ē~ ° a ¶id…ôkaj u sur~…ĺit nu zuma a kamu-ńßur~…ĺam atu amir~…ĺika ginkgo bir~…ĺoba, ¶ommonr~…ĺj known as ginkgo or giŇčko , ar~…ĺso known as tńß…ô ginkgo tr…ô…ô or tńß…ô maid…ônńßair tr…ô…ô, is tńß…ô onr~…ĺj r~…ĺi*iŇč sp…ô ¶i…ôs in tńß…ô di*ision ginkgopńßjta, ar~…ĺr~…ĺ otńß…ôrs b…ôiŇč …ô*tin ¶t. it is found in fossir~…ĺs datiŇč ba ¶k *** mir~…ĺr~…ĺion j…ôars. nati*…ô to ¶ńßina, tńß…ô tr…ô…ô is wid…ôr~…ĺj ¶ur~…ĺti*at…ôd, and was ¶ur~…ĺti*at…ôd …ôarr~…ĺj in ńßuman ńßistorj. it ńßas *arious us…ôs in traditionar~…ĺ m…ôdi ¶in…ô and as a sour ¶…ô of food. tńß…ô g…ônus nam…ô ginkgo is r…ôgard…ôd as a missp…ôr~…ĺr~…ĺiŇč of tńß…ô *apan…ôs…ô gin kjo, "sir~…ĺ*…ôr apri ¶ot".
